interest: Skeptical stakeholders (employers, conservatives, policy realists, independent researchers, non-state maintainers) share an interest in not committing to a nationwide reform whose central equity mechanism depends on infrastructure that does not yet exist and may not be buildable to spec.
value: This is feasibility/epistemic honesty in tension with the equity ambition the quota is meant to deliver — the claim insists you cannot bank an equity gain on an unbuilt tool.
fear: The anticipated harm is a 'theatrical' or 'paper' reform that ships an unvalidated, gameable proxy, fails to change who actually gets admitted, and either entrenches informal selection under a new label or discredits equity reform when the tool breaks.
affected: low-SES and Roma pupils (intended beneficiaries who bear the cost of a failed mechanism)
state-maintained schools and teachers (subject to compliance audits on an unbuilt system)
non-state maintainers (asked to align with a rule-set resting on missing infrastructure)
assumption: It assumes that a gaming-resistant, validated SES indicator is a genuine precondition for the quota to function — i.e., that no adequate proxy can be constructed and validated within the reform's timeline, and that resolving this precedes any decision to roll out.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is largely settled that no validated, gaming-resistant SES indicator currently exists in Hungarian administrative data (flagged as 'weak evidence'/'high cost, novel system'), but whether one can be built to acceptable false-positive/false-negative rates is genuinely unknown and only a costed multi-year pilot would resolve it.
